title: Blood Dried on Carpet
stainChemistry: Dried blood has undergone protein denaturation and oxidation. On carpet, the hemoglobin cross-links with the fiber polymer, and the iron component oxidizes to form rust-colored iron oxide. The protein matrix becomes insoluble and forms a tough film. The older the stain, the more cross-linking has occurred, and the harder removal becomes. Heat exposure at any point makes this nearly permanent.
whyThisWorks: Extended enzyme treatment (where fiber-safe) slowly breaks down the cross-linked protein matrix on carpet. Cold water throughout — any heat further denatures the remaining protein. For the iron component that remains after protein removal, a reducing agent addresses the rust discoloration. Multiple treatment cycles are usually needed — dried blood rarely comes out in one pass.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Protein Formula (digestant spotter) and tamp gently with spotting brush. Work from edge to center — 5-10 min dwell time
2. Apply NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and work gently to remove loosened protein and digestant residue. Flush with cold water — 3-5 min dwell time
3. If stain persists: apply sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) for stubborn oxidized protein — 5-10 min dwell time, monitor carefully
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse to restore fabric pH — 1-2 min dwell time
5. Blot dry with clean absorbent cloth. Air dry — no heat
warnings: COLD water only throughout — heat permanently sets protein
Avoid over-wetting — extract excess moisture to prevent backing/foam damage

steps: 1. Rinse immediately with COLD water. Blot with a clean white cloth — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup cold water. Apply to stain and blot — 5-10 min dwell time
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat soap application until stain fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all soap residue
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer or any heat until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop and take to a professional if the stain has been heat-set, if multiple home treatments haven't improved it, or if the fabric is delicate/expensive
warnings: NEVER use hot water on protein stains — heat sets them permanently
Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first

neverDo: No chlorine bleach on colored carpet
Avoid over-wetting — causes backing damage and mold
fiberSensitivities: Most synthetic carpet (nylon/polyester) tolerates standard chemistry
Always blot from outside in to prevent spreading
Extract excess moisture to prevent backing damage

escalation.when: If the protein has been heat-set (hot water, dryer, or iron exposure), or if the stain is older than 48 hours without treatment, or if 3 treatment cycles haven't resolved it
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is blood dried on carpet, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaning service with truck-mount extraction

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the stain fresh and wet, or dried/crusty?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
